title: EXPOSÉ IV GROTHENDIECK GROUPS OF RINGED TOPOI

pages.title: EXPOSÉ IV GROTHENDIECK GROUPS OF RINGED TOPOI

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ IV
GROTHENDIECK GROUPS OF RINGED TOPOIby L. Illusie

pages.title: 1. Recollections and generalities on Grothendieck groups

blocks.html: 1. Recollections and generalities on Grothendieck groups

Let \(C\) be a triangulated category. Recall (I 6.3 and SGA 5 VIII 2) that a map \(f\) from \(\operatorname{ob}C\) to an abelian group \(G\) is said to be additive if we have \(f(E)=f(E')+f(E'')\) for every distinguished triangle
\[E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow E''\longrightarrow E'[1].\]

The additive maps from \(\operatorname{ob}C\) to \(G\) form an abelian group, which depends functorially on \(G\). The functor thus obtained is represented by an abelian group \(k(C)\) and a universal additive map \(\operatorname{cl}_C:\operatorname{ob}C\longrightarrow k(C)\) (written \(\operatorname{cl}\) when there is no risk of confusion).

The group \(k(C)\) depends functorially on \(C\) with respect to exact functors. If \(C,C'\) are triangulated categories, two isomorphic exact functors from \(C\) to \(C'\) induce the same homomorphism \(k(C)\longrightarrow k(C')\) ; in particular, if \(u:C\longrightarrow C'\) is an exact functor which is an equivalence of categories, \(k(u):k(C)\longrightarrow k(C')\) is an isomorphism.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.2.

Let \(C\) be a triangulated category, and \(L\in\operatorname{ob}C\).

a) For every \(n\in\mathbb Z\), we have

\[\operatorname{cl}(L[n])=(-1)^n\operatorname{cl}(L).\]

If \(L',L''\) are objects of \(C\) such that we have \(L\simeq L'\oplus L''\), then we have

\[\operatorname{cl}(L)=\operatorname{cl}(L')+\operatorname{cl}(L'').\]

b) Suppose there exists \(n\in\mathbb Z\) such that \(\displaystyle\bigoplus_{i\geq0}L[2ni]\) is representable. Then we have \(\operatorname{cl}(L)=0\).
Proof. Assertion a) follows directly from the definitions (cf. SGA 5 VIII 2). For b), it suffices to remark that we have
\[\bigoplus_{i\geq0}L[2ni]\ \simeq\ L\oplus\left(\bigoplus_{i\geq0}L[2ni]\right)[2n],\]
and to apply a).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.3.

Let \(C\) be a triangulated category, \(A\) a thick subcategory ([V] I 2.1). The inclusion and quotient functors define an exact sequence

\[k(A)\longrightarrow k(C)\longrightarrow k(C/A)\longrightarrow0.\]
Proof. See (SGA 5 VIII 3.1).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4.

Let \(A\) be an additive (resp. abelian) category, and \(f\) an additive function (1.1) on \(\operatorname{ob}K^b(A)\) (resp. \(\operatorname{ob}D^b(A)\)). For \(E\in\operatorname{ob}K^b(A)\) (resp. \(D^b(A)\)) we have

\[f(E)=\sum(-1)^if(E^i)\] \[\left(\text{resp.}\quad f(E)=\sum(-1)^if(H^i(E))\right).\]
Proof. Left as an exercise for the reader.

Let \(C\) be an abelian category and \(A\) a full subcategory of \(C\) stable under finite sums. A map \(f\) from \(\operatorname{ob}A\) to an abelian group \(G\) is said to be additive if we have \(\sum(-1)^if(E^i)=0\) for every acyclic complex \(E\) of \(C\), with bounded degrees and whose components belong to \(A\). When \(A\) is stable under kernels of epimorphisms (I 1.2), it amounts to the same to say that we have \(f(L)=f(L')+f(L'')\) for every exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow L'\longrightarrow L\longrightarrow L''\longrightarrow0\) of \(C\) whose terms belong to \(A\). The additive maps from \(\operatorname{ob}A\) to \(G\) form an abelian group depending functorially on \(G\). Let us denote by \(K^{b,\phi}(A)\) the thick subcategory of \(K^b(A)\) formed of the acyclic complexes. One easily deduces from (1.3) and (1.4) that the map
\[(1.5.1)\qquad \operatorname{cl}_A:\operatorname{ob}A\longrightarrow k\bigl(K^b(A)/K^{b,\phi}(A)\bigr)\]

induced by \(\operatorname{cl}_{K^b(A)/K^{b,\phi}(A)}\) (1.1) is a universal additive map, i.e. the pair \(\bigl(k(K^b(A)/K^{b,\phi}(A)),\operatorname{cl}_A\bigr)\) represents the functor associating to each abelian group \(G\) the abelian group of additive maps from \(\operatorname{ob}A\) to \(G\). We shall set
\[(1.5.2)\qquad k\bigl(K^b(A)/K^{b,\phi}(A)\bigr)=k(A)\]
(we shall write \(k(A,C)\) instead of \(k(A)\) when we wish to avoid any ambiguity about the ambient abelian category)(1). In particular, for \(A=C\), we have
\[(1.5.3)\qquad k(D^b(C))=k(C).\]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.6.

Let \(C\) be an abelian category, \(A\) a thick subcategory (i.e. a full subcategory stable under kernels, cokernels, extensions). Denote by \(D_A(C)\) the full subcategory of \(D(C)\) formed of the objects \(E\) such that \(H^i(E)\in\operatorname{ob}A\) for every \(i\). Then \(D_A(C)\) is a triangulated subcategory ([V] I 1.2.3) of \(D(C)\), and the canonical functor \(D^b(A)\longrightarrow D_A^b(C)=D^b(C)\cap D_A(C)\) induces an isomorphism

\[k(A)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}k(D_A^b(C)),\]

where \(k(A)\) is defined by (1.5.2).
Proof. The first assertion is an immediate consequence of the cohomology exact sequence. For the second, consider the map \(f:\operatorname{ob}D_A^b(C)\longrightarrow k(A)\) defined by
\[f(E)=\sum(-1)^i\operatorname{cl}_A(H^i(E)),\]
where \(\operatorname{cl}_A\) is the map (1.5.1). By the cohomology exact sequence, \(f\) is additive (1.1), hence defines a homomorphism
\[\varphi:k(D_A^b(C))\longrightarrow k(A).\]
It follows at once from (1.4) that \(\varphi\) is inverse to the canonical homomorphism \(k(A)\longrightarrow k(D_A^b(C))\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 1.6.1.

In the situation of (1.6), the canonical functor \(D^b(A)\longrightarrow D_A^b(C)\) is not in general an equivalence of categories, as is shown by the example where \(S\) is a scheme, \(C\) the category of \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules, \(A\) the category of quasi-coherent \(\mathcal O_S\)-Modules (II Ap. I 0.3). See however the criterion (II 2.1.3).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.7.

Let \(C\) be a triangulated (resp. abelian) category, and \(C'\) a subset of \(\operatorname{ob}C\). We shall say that \(C'\) is an exact subset (cf. EGA III 3.1.1) if, for every distinguished triangle \(E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow E''\longrightarrow E'[1]\) (resp. every exact sequence \(0\longrightarrow E'\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow E''\longrightarrow0\)) such that two of the terms \(E',E,E''\) belong to \(C'\), the third belongs to it as well. We shall say that \(C'\) is dense in \(\operatorname{ob}C\) if \(\operatorname{ob}C\) is the smallest exact subset of \(\operatorname{ob}C\) containing \(C'\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.8.

Let \(C\) be a triangulated (resp. abelian) category and \(C'\) a dense subset of \(\operatorname{ob}C\). Then the elements \(\operatorname{cl}_C(E)\), for \(E\in C'\), generate \(k(C)\).

Proof. Set \(C'=C_0\), and, for \(i\geq1\), define \(C_i\) inductively as the set of objects of \(C\) appearing in a distinguished triangle (resp. a short exact sequence) two of whose terms belong to \(C_{i-1}\). The \(C_i\) form an increasing filtration of \(\operatorname{ob}C\), which, by hypothesis, is exhaustive. For \(i\geq0\), denote by \(k(C)_i\) the subgroup of \(k(C)\) generated by the elements \(\operatorname{cl}_C(E)\) for \(E\in C_i\). It is immediate from the definitions that we have, for every \(i\geq0\), \(k(C)_i=k(C)_{i+1}\), hence \(k(C)_i=k(C)_0\). Now let \(x\in k(C)\) ; one can write, as is easily seen, \(x=\operatorname{cl}(E')-\operatorname{cl}(E'')\) for \(E',E''\in\operatorname{ob}C\) (in the non-resp. case one can even arrange that \(E''=0\)). Since the filtration \((C_i)\) is exhaustive, there exists an integer \(i\geq0\) such that \(E'\) and \(E''\) belong to \(C_i\) ; we then have \(x\in k(C)_i=k(C)_0\) and we are done.

blocks.title: Exercise
blocks.html:
Exercise 1.9.

Let \(C\) be an abelian category, \(A\) a full subcategory stable under finite sums, subobjects and quotients, and such that \(\operatorname{ob}A\) is dense in \(\operatorname{ob}C\). Then the homomorphism \(k(A,C)\longrightarrow k(C)\) defined by the additive function \(E\longmapsto\operatorname{cl}_C(E)\) on \(\operatorname{ob}A\) (cf. (1.5)) is an isomorphism.

Hint: Set \(A=A_0\) and define \(A_i\), for \(i\geq1\), as the full subcategory of \(C\) formed of the objects of \(C\) which are extensions of two objects of \(C_{i-1}\). Prove that \(A_i\) satisfies the same hypotheses as \(A\), then show that the canonical homomorphism \(\varinjlim k(A_i,C)\longrightarrow k(C)\) is an isomorphism (the desired conclusion follows at once).

footnotes.html: The notation \(k(A)\) had been introduced in (I 6.3) to denote the group \(k(K^b(A))\), which is not in general isomorphic to the group defined by (1.5.2). We therefore change convention here: in the present exposé the notation \(k(A)\) will denote exclusively the group defined by (1.5.2).

pages.title: 2. The functors \(K_\bullet\) and \(K'\) of a ringed topos

blocks.html: 2. The functors \(K_\bullet\) and \(K'\) of a ringed topos
Let \((X,A)\) be a ringed topos. Recall the following notations:

\[ \begin{aligned} D^b(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}}&=\text{ catégorie des complexes pseudo-cohérents et à cohomologie}\\ &\quad\text{bornée de \(A\)-Modules à gauche (I 2.15),}\\ D(A^X)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}&=\text{ catégorie des complexes de \(A\)-Modules à gauche d'amplitude}\\ &\quad\text{parfaite finie (I 5.8.4),}\\ \operatorname{Loclib}(A^X)&=\text{ catégorie des \(A\)-Modules à gauche localement facteurs}\\ &\quad\text{directs de Modules libres de type fini (II 2.0),}\\ \operatorname{Coh}(A^X)&=\text{ catégorie des \(A\)-Modules à gauche cohérents (relativement}\\ &\quad\text{à la catégorie des \(A\)-Modules libres de type fini) (I 3.1).} \end{aligned} \]

The last two categories are full subcategories of the category \(\operatorname{Mod}(A^X)\) of left \(A\)-Modules, the first two full triangulated subcategories of \(D(A^X)=D(\operatorname{Mod}(A^X))\).

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.2.

Let \((X,A)\) be a ringed topos. Unless otherwise stated, we shall set

\[ K_\bullet(A^X)=k(D^b(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}}),\qquad K'(A^X)=k(D(A^X)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}), \]

where \(k(\ )\) is defined in (1.1), and

\[ K_{\bullet,\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)=k(\operatorname{Coh}(A^X)),\qquad K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)=k(\operatorname{Loclib}(A^X)), \]

where \(k(\ )\) is defined by (1.5.2), the ambient abelian category being \(\operatorname{Mod}(A^X)\)(1). We shall say that \(K_\bullet(A^X)\) (resp. \(K'(A^X)\)) is the Grothendieck group of pseudo-coherent (resp. perfect) complexes of left \(A\)-Modules, and that \(K_{\bullet,\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)\) (resp. \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)\)) is the Grothendieck group of coherent (resp. locally free) left \(A\)-Modules.

blocks.title: Remarks
blocks.html:
Remarks 2.3.

a) One can define variants of \(K_\bullet(\ )\) and \(K'(\ )\) by setting

\[ K_\bullet^{\mathrm{loc}}(A^X)=k(D^{\mathrm{locb}}(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}}) \quad\text{(où « locb » signifie « à cohomologie localement bornée »),} \] \[ K'_{\mathrm{loc}}(A^X)=k(D(A^X)_{\mathrm{parf}}). \]

These groups coincide with those defined in (2.2) when the final object of \(X\) is quasi-compact, but in general lend themselves less well to the computation of intersections.

b) The group \(K_{\bullet,\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)\) is of practically no interest unless \(A\) is coherent (otherwise there may well be very few coherent sheaves ...).

c) Considering the \(k\) of triangulated categories that are "too big" offers no interest. Thus, from (1.2 b)) one easily deduces the relations

\[ k(D(A^X))=k(D^-(A^X))=k(D^+(A^X))=k(D^b(A^X)) =k(D^-(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}})=0 \]

(for the last equality, observe that \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) implies \(\displaystyle\bigoplus_{i\geq0}L[2i]\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\)).
2.4. Case of a coherent sheaf of rings.

Let \((X,A)\) be a ringed topos, where \(A\) is assumed to be coherent as a left \(A\)-Module (I 3.1 and 3.6). Every coherent left \(A\)-Module is a pseudo-coherent complex (I 3.5), so we have a fully faithful functor
\[(2.4.1)\qquad \operatorname{Coh}(A^X)\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow D^b(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}}.\]

Now (I 3.4) \(\operatorname{Coh}(A^X)\) is a thick subcategory of \(\operatorname{Mod}(A^X)\), and, by virtue of (I 3.5), \(D^b(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) is the full subcategory of \(D^b(A^X)\) formed of the objects \(L\) such that \(H^i(L)\in\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Coh}(A^X)\) for every \(i\). Consequently, it follows from (1.6) that the homomorphism
\[(2.4.2)\qquad K_{\bullet,\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(A^X)\]
defined by (2.4.1) is an isomorphism.
2.5. The homomorphism \(K'\longrightarrow K_\bullet\).
Let \((X,A)\) be a ringed topos. The inclusion functor \(D(A^X)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow D^b(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) defines a homomorphism
\[(2.5.1)\qquad K'(A^X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(A^X).\]
This is in general neither injective nor surjective. However, let us make the following hypotheses:
(i) the final object of \(X\) is quasi-compact;
(ii) \(X\) has enough points, and, for every point \(x\) of \(X\), every left \(A_x\)-module is of finite tor-dimension. Then we have, by (I 5.10),
\[D(A^X)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}=D^b(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}},\]
and the homomorphism (2.5.1) is an isomorphism.
Hypotheses (i) and (ii) are satisfied for example in the case of a compact space ringed in regular local rings.
2.6. Pairings between \(K'\) and \(K'\) (non-commutative case).
Let
\[f:(X',A')\longrightarrow(X,A)\]

be a morphism of ringed topoi. Denote by \(\operatorname{Mod}_{A',f^{-1}(A)}(X')\) the category of bi-Modules, left over \(A'\), right over \(f^{-1}(A)\), and by \(\operatorname{Loclib}_{A'}(X'_{A})\) the full subcategory formed of the objects belonging to \(\operatorname{Loclib}(A'^{X'})\). By analogy with (2.2), we shall set
\[ K'_{\mathrm{naif},A'}(X'_{A})= k(\operatorname{Loclib}_{A'}(X'_{A})). \]
The tensor product

\[ \begin{aligned} \otimes_A:\operatorname{Mod}_{A',f^{-1}(A)}(X')\times\operatorname{Mod}(A^X) &\longrightarrow\operatorname{Mod}(A'^{X'}),\\ (E',E)&\longmapsto E'\otimes_{f^{-1}(A)}f^{-1}(E) \end{aligned} \]
induces a functor
\[(2.6.1)\qquad \operatorname{Loclib}_{A'}(X'_{A})\times\operatorname{Loclib}(A^X) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Loclib}(A'^{X'}). \]
The map \((E',E)\longmapsto\operatorname{cl}(E'\otimes E)\) deduced from (2.6.1) is biadditive in the sense of (1.5), and consequently defines a homomorphism
\[(2.6.2)\qquad K'_{\mathrm{naif},A'}(X'_{A})\otimes K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A^X) \longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A'^{X'}). \]

Denote by \(D_{A',f^{-1}(A)}(X')\) the derived category of \(\operatorname{Mod}_{A',f^{-1}(A)}(X')\), by \(D_{A',f^{-1}(A)}(X')_{A'\text{-}\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) the full subcategory formed of the complexes of finite perfect amplitude rel. to \(A'\) (i.e. as objects of \(D(A'^{X'})\)), and set, as in (2.2),
\[ K'_{A'}(X'_{A})= k(D_{A',f^{-1}(A)}(X')_{A'\text{-}\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}). \]
The inclusion functors define in an obvious way homomorphisms

\[ \begin{aligned} (2.6.3.1)\qquad&K'_{\mathrm{naif},A'}(X'_{A})\longrightarrow K'_{A'}(X'_{A}),\\ (2.6.3.2)\qquad&K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)\longrightarrow K'(A^X),\\ (2.6.3.3)\qquad&K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A'^{X'})\longrightarrow K'(A'^{X'}). \end{aligned} \]
We know on the other hand (I 4.18, 4.19) that the derived functor of \(\otimes_A\)
\[ \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}: D^-_{A',f^{-1}(A)}(X')\times D^-(A^X)\longrightarrow D^-(A'^{X'}) \]
induces an (exact) functor
\[(2.6.4)\qquad D_{A',f^{-1}(A)}(X')_{A'\text{-}\underline{\mathrm{parf}}} \times D(A^X)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}} \longrightarrow D(A'^{X'})_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}. \]
The latter defines a homomorphism
\[(2.6.5)\qquad K'_{A'}(X'_{A})\otimes K'(A^X)\longrightarrow K'(A'^{X'}). \]

Since the functor (2.6.1) is induced by (2.6.4), the homomorphisms (2.6.2) and (2.6.5) are compatible with the homomorphisms (2.6.3.1), (2.6.3.2) and (2.6.3.3); in other words we have a commutative diagram
\[(2.6.6)\qquad \xymatrix{ K'_{\mathrm{naif},A'}(X'_{A})\otimes K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A^X) \ar[r]\ar[d]&K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A'^{X'})\ar[d]\\ K'_{A'}(X'_{A})\otimes K'(A^X)\ar[r]&K'(A'^{X'}), }\]
where the upper and lower horizontal arrows are respectively (2.6.2) and (2.6.5), and the vertical arrows are defined by (2.6.3.1), (2.6.3.2) and (2.6.3.3).
2.7. Contravariant law and ring structure on \(K'\)
We shall make explicit some important special cases of the pairings (2.6) (their direct study is in any case immediate).

a) Let \(f:(X',A')\longrightarrow(X,A)\) be a morphism of ringed topoi. The functor \(Lf^*:D^-(A^X)\longrightarrow D^-(A'^{X'})\) sends (I 4.19.1) \(D(A^X)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) into \(D(A'^{X'})_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Loclib}(A^X)\) into \(\operatorname{Loclib}(A'^{X'})\)), and defines a homomorphism
\[(2.7.1)\qquad f^*:K'(A^X)\longrightarrow K'(A'^{X'})\]
\[\left(\text{resp. }(2.7.2)\qquad f^*:K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)\longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A'^{X'}) \right),\]
which can also be interpreted as the product by \(\operatorname{cl}(A')\) in (2.6.2) (resp. (2.6.5)). We have a commutative diagram (a special case of (2.6.6))
\[ \xymatrix{ K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)\ar[r]^{f^*}\ar[d]& K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A'^{X'})\ar[d]\\ K'(A^X)\ar[r]^{f^*}&K'(A'^{X'}), }\]
where the left and right vertical arrows are respectively (2.6.3.2) and (2.6.3.3).
Let \(g:(X,A)\longrightarrow(X'',A'')\) be a morphism of ringed topoi; the transitivity of the inverse image functors implies that the homomorphisms of type (2.7.1) (resp. 2.7.2) satisfy the relation
\[(2.7.3)\qquad (gf)^*=f^*g^*.\]
Thus \(K'(A^X)\) (resp. \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(A^X)\)) is a contravariant functor of \((X,A)\).

b) Let \((X,\mathcal O_X)\) be a commutatively ringed topos. Thanks to the associativity and commutativity of the tensor product, the homomorphism (2.6.5) (resp. (2.6.2)) (where we take \(A=\mathcal O_X\), \((X',A')=(X,A)\), \(f=\operatorname{Id}\)) endows \(K'(X)\) (resp. \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\)) with the structure of a commutative ring with unit (\(1=\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal O_X)\)), and the canonical homomorphism (2.6.3.2)
\[K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\longrightarrow K'(X)\]
is a homomorphism of unital rings.

Let \(f:(X,\mathcal O_X)\longrightarrow(Y,\mathcal O_Y)\) be a morphism of topoi ringed in commutative rings. Then the compatibility of the tensor product with inverse images shows that the homomorphism
\[ f^*:K'(Y)\longrightarrow K'(X) \quad\left(\text{resp. }f^*:K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(Y)\longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\right) \]
is a homomorphism of unital rings.
2.8. Augmentation from \(K'\) to \(H^0(\ ,\mathbb Z)\).
Let \((X,\mathcal O_X)\) be a locally ringed topos (I 2.15.1). Recall that we defined (I 6.11 to 6.14) a homomorphism of (unital) rings
\[i:H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X),\]

thus endowing \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\) (and consequently also \(K'(X)\) via the canonical homomorphism \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\longrightarrow K'(X)\)) with the structure of an algebra (with unit) over \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\). Moreover (loc. cit.) the algebra \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\) (resp. \(K'(X)\)) is augmented to \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\) by the rank, and the canonical homomorphism \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\longrightarrow K'(X)\) is a homomorphism of augmented algebras.

Let \(f:(X,\mathcal O_X)\longrightarrow(Y,\mathcal O_Y)\) be a morphism of locally ringed topoi. The homomorphisms \(f^*:K'(Y)\longrightarrow K'(X)\) and \(f^*:K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(Y)\longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\) are compatible (I 6.12, 6.13) with the preceding augmented algebra structures, which is expressed by the commutativity of the diagram (where \(\operatorname{rg}\) denotes the rank):

\[ \xymatrix{ H^0(Y,\mathbb Z)\ar[r]^i\ar[d]&K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(Y)\ar[r]\ar[d]& K'(Y)\ar[r]^{\operatorname{rg}}\ar[d]&H^0(Y,\mathbb Z)\ar[d]\\ H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\ar[r]^i&K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\ar[r]& K'(X)\ar[r]^{\operatorname{rg}}&H^0(X,\mathbb Z). }\]

* It will be seen later in the Seminar (V 3.9.1 and VI ) that \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\) can be endowed with a structure richer than that of an algebra augmented to \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\), namely with the structure of a \(\lambda\)-algebra augmented to \(H^0(X,\mathbb Z)\) regarded as a binomial ring (V 2.8), this structure being compatible with the inverse image homomorphisms. Deligne [5] has recently proved that \(K'(X)\) can be endowed with an analogous structure (at least if \(X\) is of characteristic \(p\geq0\)) and that the canonical homomorphism \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\longrightarrow K'(X)\) is a homomorphism of augmented \(\lambda\)-algebras, thus answering in the affirmative a conjecture of Grothendieck (XIV I).*
2.9. Comparison of \(K'\) and \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}\).
Let \((X,A)\) be a ringed topos. There is no reason in general for the canonical homomorphism
\[K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\longrightarrow K'(X)\]
to be injective or surjective. However, the "criteria for the existence of global resolutions" of Exp. II show that it is an isomorphism in the following cases:
(i) \(X\) is the Zariski topos of a divisorial scheme, separated or noetherian (and \(A=\mathcal O_X\)) (II 2.2.9);
(ii) \(X\) is the topos defined by a compact space and \(A\) is a soft sheaf (II 2.3.2);
(iii) \(X\) is a Stein topos whose final object is quasi-compact and \(A=\mathcal O_X\) (II 2.4.5).
2.10. Pairing between \(K'\) and \(K_\bullet\).
Let \((X,\mathcal O_X)\) be a commutatively ringed topos. We know (I 5.8.4 b)) that we have a pairing
\[ \mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}: D(X)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\times D^b(X)_{\mathrm{coh}} \longrightarrow D^b(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}. \]
This defines a homomorphism
\[(2.10.1)\qquad K'(X)\otimes K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X). \]

Thanks to the associativity of the tensor product, one sees that (2.10.1) endows \(K_\bullet(X)\) with the structure of a \(K'(X)\)-module. More generally, if \(f:(X,\mathcal O_X)\longrightarrow(Y,\mathcal O_Y)\) is a morphism of commutatively ringed topoi, one can regard \(K_\bullet(X)\) as a \(K'(Y)\)-module via the homomorphism \(f^*:K'(Y)\longrightarrow K'(X)\).
2.11. Covariant law on \(K_\bullet\) and projection formula.

Let \((X,A)\) be a ringed topos. Unlike \(K'(A^X)\), the group \(K_\bullet(A^X)\) has no natural variance with respect to \((X,A)\). However, in Algebraic Geometry and in Analytic Geometry, \(K_\bullet(X)\) tends to be covariant in \(X\) for proper morphisms. Let us make this point precise:

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a proper and pseudo-coherent morphism (III 1.2) of quasi-compact schemes. Then, subject to the validity of the finiteness conjecture for \(f\) (III 2.1), hence in particular (III 2.2) if \(Y\) is noetherian (in which case the pseudo-coherence hypothesis on \(f\) is automatically satisfied (III 1.3)) or if \(f\) is projective, the functor \(Rf_*\) induces, by virtue of (III 2.5), a functor
\[ Rf_*:D^b(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\longrightarrow D^b(Y)_{\mathrm{coh}}. \]
This defines a homomorphism
\[(2.11.1.1)\qquad f_*:K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(Y). \]
This homomorphism is \(K'(Y)\)-linear (cf. (2.10)); in other words, for \(y\in K'(Y)\) and \(x\in K_\bullet(X)\) we have the so-called projection formula
\[(2.11.1.2)\qquad f_*(yx)=yf_*(x).\]

Indeed, if \(x=\operatorname{cl}(E)\) for \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) and \(y=\operatorname{cl}(M)\) for \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D(Y)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\), we have (III 3.7)
\[ Rf_*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}}M \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} Rf_*\bigl(E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}M\bigr). \]

Finally, if \(g:Y\longrightarrow Z\) is a morphism satisfying the same hypotheses as \(f\) and for which the finiteness conjecture (III 2.1) holds, the transitivity of the direct image functors implies, for the homomorphisms (2.11.1.1), the formula
\[(2.11.1.3)\qquad g_*f_*=(gf)_*.\]

Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a proper morphism of analytic spaces of finite dimension. Then, by the finiteness theorem of Grauert [2], \(Rf_*\) sends \(D^b(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) into \(D^b(Y)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), and therefore defines a homomorphism
\[(2.11.2.1)\qquad f_*:K_\bullet(X)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(Y). \]

Like its algebraic analogue (2.11.1.1), this is \(K'(Y)\)-linear, i.e. formula (2.11.1.2) holds, as follows from the projection formula at the level of derived categories (III 5.9 b)). We likewise have the transitivity formula (2.11.1.3).

As soon as one leaves the algebraic or analytic setting, this covariant character of \(K_\bullet\) for proper morphisms disappears. Thus, let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a proper morphism of topological spaces ringed by the sheaf of complex-valued continuous functions. Then, if \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), we do not in general have \(Rf_*(E)\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(Y)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), as is already evident for \(E=\mathcal O_X\) and \(Y\) reduced to a point. In fact, the definition (2.2) of \(K_\bullet\) seems mainly suited to the needs of Algebraic Geometry and Analytic Geometry, and a good definition of the \(K_\bullet\) of a topological space remains to be found. It seems moreover that one will have to leave the setting of \(\mathcal O_X\)-linear complexes, and define for a morphism \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) (of topological spaces, resp. of mixed manifolds (II App. II), etc.) a suitable class of complexes of topological vector sheaves on \(X\), which should encompass for example, when \(f\) is the structural morphism of a mixed manifold, the elliptic complexes on \(X\) relative to \(Y\). This is what is suggested by the finiteness theorem (II App. II), as well as by an analogous theorem (where \(Y\) is assumed reduced to a point) proved by Grothendieck in [3].
2.12. Unusual variances of \(K'\) and \(K_\bullet\).

Let \(f:(X,A)\longrightarrow(Y,B)\) be a morphism of finite tor-dimension of ringed topoi (III 3.1). The functor \(Lf^*:D^-(B^Y)\longrightarrow D^-(A^X)\) sends \(D^b(B^Y)\) into \(D^b(A^X)\), hence (I 2.16.1) \(D^b(B^Y)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) into \(D^b(A^X)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), and consequently defines a homomorphism
\[(2.12.1)\qquad f^*:K_\bullet(B^Y)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(A^X). \]

If \(A\) and \(B\) are commutative, the commutation of the inverse image with the tensor product shows that the homomorphism (2.12.1) is compatible with the module structures of \(K_\bullet(Y)\) and \(K_\bullet(X)\) over \(K'(Y)\) and \(K'(X)\) respectively (2.10); in other words, for \(a\in K'(Y)\) and \(y\in K_\bullet(Y)\), we have the formula
\[(2.12.2)\qquad f^*(ay)=af^*(y).\]
If \(g:(Y,B)\longrightarrow(Z,C)\) is a morphism of finite tor-dimension of ringed topoi, the transitivity of the inverse image functors implies that the homomorphisms (2.12.1) satisfy the formula:
\[(2.12.1.1)\qquad f^*g^*=(gf)^*.\]

Suppose now that we are in the situation (2.11.1) (resp. (2.11.2)), with \(f\) of finite tor-dimension. Then (III 4.8.1 and 5.9 b)) the functor \(Rf_*\) sends \(D(X)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) into \(D(Y)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\), and consequently defines a homomorphism
\[(2.12.3)\qquad f_*:K'(X)\longrightarrow K'(Y). \]
This is not in general a ring homomorphism. However, for \(y\in K_\bullet(Y)\) (resp. \(K'(Y)\)) and \(x\in K'(X)\), we have the formula
\[(2.12.4)\qquad f_*\bigl(f^*(y)x\bigr)=yf_*(x), \]

where \(f^*\) is the homomorphism (2.12.1) (resp. (2.7.1)). The proof is analogous to that of (2.11.1.2). If \(g:Y\longrightarrow Z\) is a morphism satisfying the same hypotheses as \(f\), the homomorphisms (2.12.3) satisfy the transitivity formula:
\[(2.12.3.1)\qquad g_*f_*=(gf)_*.\]

The homomorphism (2.12.3) is one of the essential ingredients of the Riemann-Roch theorem, in Algebraic Geometry as well as in Analytic Geometry (for indications in the analytic case, see (Exp. 0, 5)). Suppose that \(X\) and \(Y\) are separated and divisorial schemes (II 2.2.5) (which is the case for example if \(X\) and \(Y\) possess ample invertible sheaves). Then (2.9 (i)) \(K'(X)\) (resp. \(K'(Y)\)) is identified with \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\) (resp. \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(Y)\)), and (2.12.3) therefore defines a homomorphism
\[(2.12.3')\qquad f_*:K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(Y). \]

As has already been explained in (Exp. 0, 3.1), the definition of (2.12.3') was by no means obvious a priori. It even constitutes, from the point of view of our Seminar, one of the main motivations for the sorites of Exp. I to Exp. IV.

footnotes.html: We shall allow ourselves to omit the index \(A\) when no confusion can result. Sometimes also we shall drop the index "naive", but with explicit mention.

pages.title: 3. Complements on the Grothendieck groups of schemes(1)

blocks.html: 3. Complements on the Grothendieck groups of schemes(1)

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1.0.

Let

\[ \xymatrix{ X\ar[d]_f&X'\ar[l]_g\ar[d]^{f'}\\ Y&Y'\ar[l]_h } \]

be a cartesian square of schemes, where \(Y\) is quasi-compact and \(f\) quasi-compact and quasi-separated, and let \(E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X)\) be a complex with quasi-coherent cohomology. We assume \(X\) and \(Y'\) tor-independent over \(Y\) (III 1.5). We assume moreover that either \(h\) is of finite tor-dimension or \(E\) is of finite flat amplitude rel. to \(f\). Under these conditions, the base change map (cf. SGA 4 XVII 4)

\[ Lh^*Rf_*E\longrightarrow Rf'_*Lg^*E \]

is defined and is an isomorphism.
Proof: To define the map, one uses the trivial duality formula (SGA 4 XVII 2.3) for \(h\), which says that we have a canonical functorial isomorphism
\[(*)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(Lh^*L,M)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(L,Rh_*M) \]

for \(L\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(Y)\), \(M\in\operatorname{ob}D^+(Y')\). Using (III 3.5.2) and (III 3.7.1), one easily checks that we have \(Lg^*E\in\operatorname{ob}D^b(X')\) and \(Rf_*E\in\operatorname{ob}D^-(Y)\), so that applying \((*)\) with \(L=Rf_*E\) and \(M=Rf'_*Lg^*E\), we are reduced to defining a map
\[ Rf_*E\longrightarrow Rf_*Rg_*Lg^*E. \]
For the latter we take the image under \(Rf_*\) of the adjunction map
\[E\longrightarrow Rg_*Lg^*E\ ;\]

coming from the trivial duality formula for \(g\). The base change map is thus defined; it remains to show that it is an isomorphism. For this, we may first assume \(Y\) and \(Y'\) affine. Then, by the Leray spectral sequence of an open covering of \(X\), we are reduced to the case where \(X\) and \(X'\) are affine. We therefore have a cocartesian square of rings
\[ \xymatrix{ B\ar[r]&B'\\ A\ar[r]\ar[u]&A'\ar[u], } \]
and we may assume that \(E\) is defined by a bounded above complex of \(B\)-modules. The tor-independence of \(A'\) and \(B\) over \(A\), which reads
\[ B\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}A' \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}B', \]
then implies that we have in \(D(A')\)
\[ E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_A}A' \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} E\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_B}B', \]
whence the proposition.
Translating (3.1.0) in terms of Grothendieck groups, we obtain:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.1.1.

Let

\[ \xymatrix{ X\ar[d]_f&X'\ar[l]_g\ar[d]^{f'}\\ Y&Y'\ar[l]_h } \]

be a cartesian square of schemes. We assume \(Y\) and \(Y'\) quasi-compact, \(X\) and \(Y'\) tor-independent over \(Y\) (III 1.5), \(f\) proper and pseudo-coherent, and the finiteness conjecture (III 2.1) satisfied for \(f\) and \(f'\) (for example \(Y\) and \(Y'\) noetherian, or \(f\) projective). Then, if \(f\) is perfect (resp. \(h\) of finite tor-dimension), we have, for \(x\in K'(X)\) (resp. \(K_\bullet(X)\)),

\[ h^*f_*(x)=f'_*g^*(x), \]

where \(h^*,g^*\) are defined by (2.7.1) (resp. (2.12.1)), and \(f_*,f'_*\) by (2.12.3) (resp. (2.11.1.1)) (note that by virtue of (III 4.7.1) \(f'\) is perfect if \(f\) is, and that by virtue of (III 3.5.1) \(g\) is of finite tor-dimension if \(h\) is).

footnotes.html: The results of this section will not be used in Exp. V to VIII.

pages.title: 3.2. Passage to the limit on \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}\) and \(K'\)

blocks.html: 3.2. Passage to the limit on \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}\) and \(K'\)

Let \((X_i,f_{ij})_{i\in I}\) be a filtered projective system of quasi-compact and quasi-separated schemes, the transition morphisms being affine (cf. EGA IV 8.2.2). Set \(X=\varprojlim X_i\), and denote by \(f_i:X\longrightarrow X_i\) the canonical homomorphism.
The homomorphisms (2.7.2) \(f_i^*:K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X_i)\longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\) define, thanks to the transitivity formula (2.7.3), a homomorphism
\[(3.2.1.1)\qquad \varinjlim K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X_i)\longrightarrow K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X). \]

I claim that the homomorphism (3.2.1.1) is an isomorphism. Indeed, let \(E\) be a locally free \(\mathcal O_X\)-Module of finite type. By (EGA IV 8.5.2 and 8.5.5), there exist an index \(i\) and a locally free \(\mathcal O_{X_i}\)-Module of finite type \(E_i\) such that we have \(E\simeq f_i^*(E_i)\). By virtue of (EGA IV 8.5.2.5), the image of \(\operatorname{cl}(E_i)\) in \(\varinjlim K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X_i)\) does not depend on the choice of \(E_i\); let us denote it by \(u(E)\). By (EGA IV 8.5.2 and 8.5.6) the map
\[ u:\operatorname{ob}\operatorname{Loclib}(X)\longrightarrow \varinjlim K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X_i) \]

thus defined is additive (1.5) ; it therefore defines a homomorphism \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\longrightarrow\varinjlim K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X_i)\), which one checks at once, thanks to (EGA IV 8.5.2), to be inverse to (3.2.1.1), which proves our assertion.
One defines in the same way a canonical homomorphism
\[(3.2.2.1)\qquad \varinjlim K'(X_i)\longrightarrow K'(X). \]

Using Deligne's localization technique (SGA 4 XVII), which implies that the category of perfect complexes on \(X\) is equivalent to the category \(\varinjlim\) of the analogous categories \(\operatorname{Parf}(X_i)\), one finds again that (3.2.2.1) is an isomorphism.

Suppose the \(X_i\) and \(X\) noetherian, and the morphisms \(f_{ij}\) flat (the same then holds for the \(f_i\)). Then the homomorphisms (2.12.1) \(f_i^*:K_\bullet(X_i)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X)\) define an isomorphism
\[(3.2.2.1)\qquad \varinjlim K_\bullet(X_i)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}K_\bullet(X). \]
The proof is analogous to that of (3.2.1), using the fact (2.4) that \(K_\bullet(X)\simeq K_{\bullet,\mathrm{naif}}(X)\) can be computed in terms of coherent sheaves on \(X\).

Since the module structures of the \(K_\bullet(X_i)\) over the \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X_i)\) are compatible with the inverse images \(f_{ij}^*\) by (2.7 a)) and (2.12.2), we find, by passage to the limit, that \(\varinjlim K_\bullet(X_i)\) is endowed with a module structure over \(\varinjlim K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X_i)\); the latter is compatible, via the isomorphisms (3.2.1.1) and (3.2.2.1), with the module structure of \(K_\bullet(X)\) over \(K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\); in other words we have a commutative diagram, in which the vertical arrows are defined by (3.2.1.1) and (3.2.2.1):

\[ \xymatrix{ \varinjlim K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X_i)\otimes\varinjlim K_\bullet(X_i)\ar[r]\ar[d]_\sim& \varinjlim K_\bullet(X_i)\ar[d]^\sim\\ K'_{\mathrm{naif}}(X)\otimes K_\bullet(X)\ar[r]&K_\bullet(X). } \]
3.3. Relative Grothendieck groups
Let \(f:X\longrightarrow Y\) be a locally of finite type morphism of schemes. We shall set:
\[ K'(f)=k(D(f)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}),\qquad K_\bullet(f)=k(D^b(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}), \]

where \(D^b(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) denotes the category of complexes which are pseudo-coherent rel. to \(f\) and with bounded cohomology (III 1.2), and \(D(f)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) the category of complexes of finite perfect amplitude rel. to \(f\) (III 4.2). When there is no possible ambiguity, we shall sometimes write \(K'(X/Y)\) (resp. \(K_\bullet(X/Y)\)) instead of \(K'(f)\) (resp. \(K_\bullet(f)\)).

The groups \(K'(f)\) and \(K_\bullet(f)\) do not possess the rich structure of their "absolute" analogues \(K'(X)\) and \(K_\bullet(X)\), since the tensor product does not in general define a homomorphism \(K'(f)\otimes K'(f)\longrightarrow K'(f)\) (resp. \(K'(f)\otimes K_\bullet(f)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(f)\)). On the other hand, they satisfy interesting variance properties, which we shall now review.
Let
\[ \xymatrix{ X\ar[r]^f\ar[dr]_h&Y\ar[d]^g\\ &S } \]
be a commutative triangle of locally of finite type morphisms of schemes.
If \(g\) is pseudo-coherent, we have \(K_\bullet(f)=K_\bullet(h)\) (III 1.12). If \(g\) is smooth and \(X\) quasi-compact, we have \(K'(f)=K'(h)\) (III 4.4.1).
By (III 1.11 and 4.5), the functor \(\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_Y}\) defines pairings

\[ D(f)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\times D^b(g)_{\mathrm{coh}} \longrightarrow D^b(h)_{\mathrm{coh}}, \] \[ D(f)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\times D(g)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}} \longrightarrow D(h)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}, \]
which induce homomorphisms
\[ K'(f)\otimes K_\bullet(g)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(h), \] \[ K'(f)\otimes K'(g)\longrightarrow K'(h). \]

We deduce in particular (by taking \(X=Y\), \(f=\operatorname{Id}\)) that \(K_\bullet(g)\) is a \(K'(Y)\)-module. On the other hand, if \(f\) is perfect, the product by \(\operatorname{cl}(\mathcal O_X)\in K'(f)\) defines \(K'(Y)\)-linear homomorphisms
\[ f^*:K_\bullet(Y/S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(X/S), \qquad\text{et}\qquad f^*:K'(Y/S)\longrightarrow K'(X/S). \]

Suppose \(S\) quasi-compact, \(X\) and \(Y\) of finite type over \(S\), and \(f\) proper. Then, subject to the validity of the finiteness conjecture (III 2.1), hence in particular if \(Y\) is noetherian or if \(f\) is projective, the functor \(Rf_*\) sends \(D^b(h)_{\mathrm{coh}}\) into \(D^b(g)_{\mathrm{coh}}\), hence defines a homomorphism
\[ f_*:K_\bullet(X/S)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(Y/S). \]
If moreover \(f\) is perfect, \(Rf_*\) sends \(D(h)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) into \(D(g)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) (III 4.8), and defines a homomorphism
\[ f_*:K'(X/S)\longrightarrow K'(Y/S). \]
From the projection formula (III 3.7) we deduce the formula
\[ f_*(ax)=af_*(x) \]
for \(f\) proper, \(a\in K'(S)\), \(x\in K_\bullet(X)\) (resp. \(f\) proper and perfect, \(a\in K_\bullet(S)\), \(x\in K'(X/S)\)).

Suppose \(S\) noetherian, \(X\) and \(Y\) of finite type over \(S\), and \(f\) compactifiable and perfect. Then (III 4.9.1) the functor \(f^!\) sends \(D(g)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\) into \(D(h)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\), and defines a homomorphism
\[ f^!:K'(Y/S)\longrightarrow K'(X/S). \]
Under the hypotheses of (III 4.7), the functor \(\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathbf L}_S}\) defines pairings

\[ D(f_1)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\times D^b(f_2)_{\mathrm{coh}} \longrightarrow D^b(f)_{\mathrm{coh}}, \] \[ D(f_1)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}\times D(f_2)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}} \longrightarrow D(f)_{\underline{\mathrm{parf}}}, \]
which induce homomorphisms
\[ K'(f_1)\otimes K_\bullet(f_2)\longrightarrow K_\bullet(f), \] \[ K'(f_1)\otimes K'(f_2)\longrightarrow K'(f). \]
(Of course, for \(X_i=Y_i=S\), we recover the \(K'(S)\)-module structure of \(K_\bullet(S)\).)
If \(X\) is flat over \(S\), we deduce from this, for \(n\geq1\), homomorphisms
\[ K'(X/S)^{\otimes n}\longrightarrow K'((X/S)^n), \]
which should in a sense replace the missing ring structure on \(K'(X/S)\).
BIBLIOGRAPHY

[1] Deligne, P., papiers secrets.

[2] Grauert, H., Ein Theorem der Analytischen Garbentheorie ...,
Pub. Math. IHES, n°5 (1960).

[3] Grothendieck, A., Théorèmes de finitude pour la cohomologie des
faisceaux, Bull. Soc. Math. France, 84, 1956, p.1-7.

[V] Verdier, J.-L., Catégories dérivées, IHES (1964), mimeographed.
